title: Gan Fa
author: Kazuo Inamori
summary: This book reframes work, discipline, and long-term commitment in a way that makes serious effort feel less like a slogan and more like a method.
takeaways: A lot of growth comes from doing ordinary work with unusual consistency.
Work is not only how we make a living; it is also where judgement and character get shaped.

title: Huo Fa
author: Kazuo Inamori
summary: It connects altruism, self-discipline, and personal cultivation, reminding me that growth is not only about ability but also about values and how one lives.
takeaways: Long-term thinking is not just about waiting; it is about repeatedly correcting attitude and choice.
The way you build products is deeply shaped by the kind of person you are becoming.

title: The 7 Habits of Highly Effective People
author: Stephen R. Covey
summary: From proactivity to first-things-first and win-win thinking, this book keeps pulling me back toward building a sustainable system for personal effectiveness.
takeaways: Real effectiveness is less about filling the schedule and more about protecting what matters before it becomes urgent.
Growth and collaboration both work better when they sit on principles instead of tricks.

title: This World Is Both Cruel and Tender
author: Justin Sun
summary: This book is strongly shaped by personal ambition and the context of its era, which made me think more about execution, drive, and narrative at the same time.
takeaways: In an era that feels both harsh and generous, steady action often decides the gap.
Personal narrative becomes powerful when it aligns with timing and opportunity.

title: Yu Jun's Product Methodology
author: Yu Jun
summary: This book helps me connect user value, transaction models, and product decisions into one way of thinking about what to build and why.
takeaways: Products are not feature piles; they are ongoing tradeoffs between user value and business value.
Better product work requires understanding both user models and transaction models.

title: Thinking, Fast and Slow
author: Daniel Kahneman
summary: It helps me re-examine decision-making, bias, and judgement through the lens of fast and slow thinking, which matters a lot in both product work and user understanding.
takeaways: Many decisions that look rational are shaped much earlier by intuition and bias.
In product work, I need to watch both my own mental shortcuts and the ones users bring with them.

title: Hacking Growth
author: Fan Bing
summary: It breaks growth into a coordination problem across product, technology, data, and distribution, which keeps reminding me that growth should be built into the product itself.
takeaways: Growth should live inside the product structure instead of being attached later as marketing.
Low-cost growth depends on repeatable experiments that can actually be tested and learned from.

title: Contagious
author: Jonah Berger
summary: This book examines why ideas spread, which is highly relevant to how I package products, write publicly, and design hooks for communication.
takeaways: People often share ideas not only for the information itself, but to express something about themselves.
If an idea needs to travel, it has to be designed so it is easier to retell and easier to act on.
